segments.title: Fairmile D Dog Boats in Britain's coastal fleet
segments.summary: An introduction to the Fairmile D Dog Boat and the Fairmile yard's importance to the Royal Navy's wartime coastal fleet.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke introduces the Fairmile D, known as the Dog Boat, as a particularly important type among Britain's small coastal combatants. He places it in the wider Fairmile output of motor torpedo boats, motor gun boats, and motor launches that supplied much of the Royal Navy's World War II force. The more familiar Fairmile B variants provide the comparison that makes the D's scale stand out.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the Fairmile D and describes Fairmile's output of coastal craft for the Royal Navy.

segments.title: A 109-ton coastal fighting craft
segments.summary: The Fairmile D's 109-ton displacement and heavier armament distinguish it from the smaller craft usually associated with coastal forces.
segments.body: The Dog Boat is presented as the large and powerful member of the Fairmile family rather than another small launch. At 109 tons, Dr. Clarke says it outweighed an E-boat and carried substantially more firepower. That comparison helps explain why the D could fill a more forceful motor-gun-boat role than its size might suggest.

evidence.note: The transcript calls the D the big, powerful boat and gives its 109-ton displacement, E-boat comparison, and firepower.

segments.title: Guns, torpedoes, and D-Day coverage
segments.summary: Some Dog Boats exchanged part of their gun armament for torpedoes, yet retained enough firepower for the coastal-force tasks needed around D-Day.
segments.body: Later in the war, the changing demand for motor torpedo boats led to some Fairmile Ds receiving torpedoes in place of part of their gun battery. Dr. Clarke stresses that the conversions did not turn them into lightly armed craft: substantial gun power remained necessary. The result was a flexible coastal vessel whose armament could be adjusted for the demands of D-Day coverage.

evidence.note: The transcript describes replacing some guns with torpedoes while retaining firepower for D-Day coastal-force duties.

segments.title: Why not a modern Dog Boat?
segments.summary: A closing comparison asks why calls for a modern Flower-class corvette are not matched by calls for a modern Fairmile D Dog Boat.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke ends with a force-design question rather than a simple historical comparison. When people advocate a modern Flower-class corvette, he asks why the Fairmile D is not proposed as an alternative model. The contrast invites viewers to think about which wartime capabilities, not just which famous hull types, a modern coastal force might need.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts calls for a modern Flower-class corvette with the idea of a modern Fairmile D Dog Boat.
